title: Chapter 36: The Return
content: Eight months.

Eight months since the glitch that rewired the world, since blockchain ledgers hemorrhaged secrets across a thousand screens, since Vivienne Sterling and Jacqueline Vance dissolved into salt air and silence. Eight months since I made the only choice that let me sleep at night.
And now my book is on the bestseller list.

I hold the hardcover in my hands — the ninth printing, they tell me, which is obscene — and I breathe in. Paper and ink, that chemical sweetness of fresh-pressed pages, the faintly metallic tang of the jacket laminate. Two hundred thousand copies. BookTok turned it into a phenomenon: girls filming themselves crying on the beach, captioning it "when fiction breaks your heart and you know it's not fiction." The reviews called it *the Gatsby for the crypto age*, which would've made me laugh if it didn't make me want to throw up.

*Gilded Glitch.* My name on the spine. My words filling the pages. A story about a woman who falls in love with a brilliant criminal, then another woman, then both of them at once, and the impossible geometry of choosing between safety and truth. Published as fiction. Protected as fiction. Every literary lawyer in the country signed off on it: you cannot subpoena a novel. You cannot cross-examine a metaphor. The First Amendment wraps around my three hundred and twelve pages like Kevlar.
But everyone knows it's true.

That's the thing no one warned me about. The legal shield works — the DOJ can't touch it, no grand jury can compel me to testify about events I've already sworn are fictional. But the knowledge isn't contained. It bleeds. Every reader who finishes the last chapter and whispers *that really happened* becomes a carrier. The truth is out there, viral and alive, wearing the mask of a story. I built the perfect hiding place: a fact so well-disguised as fiction that the law itself looks the other way.

I set the book down. My apartment smells like coffee and the lavender candle Cassandra sent after the Times review ran. Life goes on. The apartment is clean, the plants are alive, the book deal paid off my student loans. I have everything I said I wanted.
Funny how that works.
I don't know Agent Reyes. I've never met her, never seen her face, never heard her voice. But I know her from the way she reads.
Because I know she's reading.

A contact at the Bureau — one of Tom Hargrove's old colleagues who still owes him a favor, still passes him quiet intelligence like coins across a table — told us. Agent Elena Reyes. San Francisco field office. Cyber-crimes division. She requested a copy of *Gilded Glitch* through official channels two months ago, and she's been reading it the way a coroner reads a body: slowly, methodically, cataloguing every mark.

I can picture it — I've been picturing it for weeks. The hardcover open on her desk, bright yellow sticky notes fanning out from the margins like small flags of warning. *Timeline check.* Written in her precise hand, pressed against the page where my narrator describes the night Jax first accessed the private ledger. *Verify wallet routing.* Slapped next to the paragraph about the offshore transfers that I'd disguised as a fictional scheme but reconstructed from memory with surgical accuracy. *Cross-reference with SF office raid.* Planted on the chapter where the characters — not people, *characters* — drove past the Federal building on Golden Gate Avenue the morning after the warrant was executed.
She's reading my novel as a case file. And the terrifying thing, the thing I should've anticipated but didn't, is that she's good.
She's finding things.

Not the big stuff — the big stuff is too well-insulated, too many layers of fictionalization between Jax's real protocols and the ones on the page. But the small details. The incidental textures I included because I'm a writer and writers steal from life: the way the VPN routed through Reykjavik before bouncing to Singapore, the specific timestamp on the server log, the name of the compliance officer who looked the other way. Fictional names, fictional timetables — but the architecture underneath is real. And Reyes has been tracing blockchain anomalies for eighteen months. She can see the skeleton through the skin.

She's closing gaps that shouldn't be closeable. Not because my fiction was sloppy, but because truth has a signature. A rhythm. You can change the notes, but the melody is recognizable to anyone who's been listening long enough.
Reyes has been listening longer than anyone.
The letter arrives on a Tuesday.

Not email. Not a phone call. A letter — cream-colored stock, heavy in my hand, the kind of paper that costs more than it should because it's meant to convey gravity. Hand-delivered to my publisher by a courier who signed for it and left without a word. My editor, Margaret, called me first: *There's something you need to see.*
I open it at my kitchen counter. The coffee goes cold.
*Dear Ms. Chen,*

*I hope this letter finds you well. I'm writing in my capacity as a Special Agent with the Federal Bureau of Investigation, San Francisco Division, regarding an ongoing investigation. We are seeking the whereabouts of Jacqueline Vance, who is wanted for questioning in connection with a series of financial irregularities dating to 2024-2025.*

*Your recent novel has come to my attention, and while I understand and respect its classification as a work of fiction, I would be grateful for the opportunity to speak with you informally about any insights you may have that could assist our investigation. This request is entirely voluntary, and you are under no obligation to respond.*
*I appreciate your time and consideration.*
*Respectfully,*

*Special Agent Elena Reyes*
*FBI San Francisco Division*

I read it three times. The first time, I'm scared. The second time, I'm impressed — it's a masterwork of bureaucratic politeness, every word measured, every implication hidden beneath a film of professionalism. *Entirely voluntary.* *No obligation.* Which means: you can say no, and we can't make you, but we'll remember that you said no.
The third time, I'm angry.

Not at Reyes. She's doing her job. I understand her better than she'd probably be comfortable knowing — we're both women who follow threads, who can't let go of a mystery, who see patterns in chaos and need to know if they're real. She read my book not because she had to but because she *needed* to. The same compulsion that made me write it made her annotate it with yellow flags.

No. I'm angry at myself. Because I left tracks. Not the ones she found — those I can live with, those are just the collateral damage of writing something true. I'm angry because I know where Jax and Vivienne are. And somewhere in my book, encoded in the precise way I described the Pacific Ocean at sunset — the angle of the light, the quality of the fog, the way the water moves against a particular kind of shore — is enough for someone like Reyes to triangulate. Not this year. Maybe not next year. But eventually.
Unless I'm better at hiding than she is at finding.

The letter from Jax arrived six months ago. Not email. Not text. Pen on paper, handwritten, the ink slightly smeared where her left hand dragged across the page — she always was a messy writer, too fast for her own good. The paper was thin, foreign, the kind of stationary you find in a pharmacy in a small coastal town where tourists don't go.
She didn't say where she was. She wouldn't. But she described the ocean.

*I watch the water every morning,* she wrote. *It's not like Malibu. The fog here doesn't burn off until almost noon, and when it does, the light comes through sideways, gold and strange, like someone tilted the whole sky. The water is colder than you'd expect for this latitude. Viv says it's the Humboldt Current. She says it like she's talking about a person she met once, someone she didn't like but respects.*
*The beach faces northwest. I know you'd notice that. You always notice which way the water goes.*
*I think of you. I think of you all the time.*
*Be safe. Be brilliant. Be free.*

I memorized it the first time I read it. Not the words — the *geography*. Northwest-facing beach. Fog that burns off at noon. Sideways gold light. Cold water, Humboldt Current. A small coastal town. Not Malibu.

I know where they are. Not the coordinates, not the name of the town — but the region. The latitude. The Current narrows it to a stretch of coast that runs maybe three hundred kilometers. And within that band, there are only a handful of towns small enough to have a single pharmacy, quiet enough that two women — one unmistakably elegant, one unmistakably brilliant — could live without drawing attention.
I know. And I will never tell.
I sit at my desk. I take out a piece of paper — not the heavy cream stock, just regular printer paper, the kind that says *nothing to see here* — and I write.
*Dear Agent Reyes,*
*Thank you for your letter. I appreciate the professionalism with which you've approached this matter.*

*I wish I could help. But my book is fiction. I made it all up. The characters, the schemes, the settings — all products of my imagination, drawn from research and conjecture, not personal knowledge. I have no information about the whereabouts of Jacqueline Vance or anyone else connected to your investigation.*
*I'm sorry I can't be of more assistance.*
*Sincerely,*
*Maya Chen*
I sign it. I fold it. I put it in an envelope. I write the address of the San Francisco field office, and I seal it, and I set it by the door for tomorrow's mail.

It's the cleanest move I've ever made. Intellectual checkmate, disguised as a polite refusal. The novel is fiction. I swore it was fiction. The publisher swore it was fiction. The copyright registration says fiction, the Library of Congress catalog says fiction, every legal document associated with *Gilded Glitch* says fiction. If I were to now acknowledge that any part of it was true — even the smallest detail — I'd be confessing to perjury, fraud, and obstruction, all at once. The Fifth Amendment shields me from having to incriminate myself, but it can't shield me from the contradiction.
So: I made it all up. The wall holds. The story protects itself.
But my hands are shaking.

Santa Monica. Late afternoon. I drive there instead of Malibu because Malibu isn't mine anymore — it's theirs, it's encrypted into the geography of Jax's letter, and going there would feel like leaving a fingerprint near a crime scene. Santa Monica is public, democratic, crowded enough to be anonymous.

The wind off the Pacific is cold. It cuts through my jacket, finds the space between my collar and my neck, and I shiver. The beach is almost empty — it's November, and the locals know better than to swim, and the tourists are at the pier taking photos of the Ferris wheel. I walk down to the waterline and stop.

The sand is damp and firm under my shoes. The tide is coming in, each wave overlapping the last, erasing footprints in real time. I can smell the salt and the kelp and the diesel from the fishing boats and underneath all of it, that clean mineral emptiness that the ocean always carries — the smell of a thing too large to be possessed.

I take out my phone. The screen glows bright in the fading light, blue-white against the grey sky, and my reflection stares back at me from the dark glass before I unlock it. I open a new message. I type in a number that doesn't exist — hasn't existed for eight months, disconnected the same day the glitch went public, a ghost line that Jax set up and burned and that no carrier has any record of.
I type:
*Reyes read the book. She's close. Be careful.*
I hit send. The message tries. The little wheel spins. Then: *Not delivered.* The red exclamation mark. The confirmation that the number is dead, has been dead, will stay dead.

I stare at it for a long time. The wind pushes my hair across my face and I don't bother to move it. The screen dims, then brightens again when my thumb shifts. *Not delivered.* Three syllables of silence.

No response. I expected that. The number is a void, a dead channel, a frequency that exists only in the memory of the phone in my hand and the woman who used to answer it. The message will never arrive. It's already failed.
But I sent it anyway.

Because somewhere — in a small town where the fog burns off at noon and the beach faces northwest and the Humboldt Current makes the water colder than it should be — Jax is watching the same ocean. The same Pacific. The same water that's touching my feet in Santa Monica is touching her shore, her beach, her morning routine of coffee and Vivienne and the quiet life they built out of the rubble I helped them escape.

And maybe — maybe — she's still listening. On some frequency I can't access, through some protocol we never discussed, in the way that people who love each other stay connected even when every rational system says the connection is severed.
The signal is out there. Blinking. Protected by a story that's true enough to matter and fictional enough to be safe.
I put my phone away. I look at the water, and the water looks back, and the wind carries something that might be salt and might be rain and might be nothing at all.
I warned a ghost. And somewhere, that ghost is listening.
Even if she can't answer.
